author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
Why the Passage Referred to Must Be Understood of a Man Established Under Grace.
title: Why the Passage Referred to Must Be Understood of a Man Established Under Grace.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And it had once appeared to me also that the apostle was in this argument of his describing a man under the law.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: But afterwards I was constrained to give up the idea by those words where he says, “Now, then, it is no more I that do it.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For to this belongs what he says subsequently also: “There is, therefore, now no condemnation to them that are in Christ Jesus.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And because I do not see how a man under the law should say, “I delight in the law of God after the inward man;” since this very delight in good, by which, moreover, he does not consent to evil, not from fear of penalty, but from love of righteousness (for this is meant by “delighting”), can only be attributed to grace.
